stem: What are the two most important properties of the Message Batches API to remember?
options: No discount, but guaranteed 1-hour completion
90% discount; 72-hour window
Roughly 50% off equivalent on-demand pricing, and up to 24 hours to complete — design SLAs around the worst case, not the typical case
Same price as real-time, but higher rate limits
rationales: There is a substantial discount, and completion can take a full day.
Both numbers are wrong — half off, one day.
Many batches finish sooner, but you cannot rely on it. The 24-hour ceiling is what SLA math must use.
The discount is the point; rate limits aren't the batch API's pitch.

stem: Which workload is a POOR fit for batch processing?
options: A user waiting interactively for each result
Nightly re-classification of the document archive
High-volume independent extractions with a 3-day deadline
Weekly summary generation for internal reports
rationales: Batch latency and interactive humans are incompatible — a person cannot wait up to 24 hours. Same for short-deadline alerts and steps that feed each other's inputs.
Delay-tolerant, independent, high-volume: the batch profile exactly.
Independent requests with generous deadlines are what the discount is for.
Nothing about a weekly report needs real-time pricing.

stem: How does an application reliably match batch results to their source records?
options: By result order — batches preserve input order
By timestamp of completion
By re-parsing each output to identify its document
By `custom_id`, never by position — use stable, unique identifiers (often the source record's primary key) so partial re-runs stay unambiguous
rationales: Results may arrive in any order — position-based matching corrupts data silently.
Completion times say nothing about which request a result answers.
Content-sniffing is fragile and fails exactly on the ambiguous cases.
`custom_id` is mandatory for reliable processing; a duplicated ID makes matching ambiguous, so derive it from something stable and unique.

stem: Results must be usable within 36 hours of each document's arrival. Batch processing may take up to 24 hours; post-processing is negligible. What is the maximum safe submission cadence?
options: Once a day — 24-hour cadence
Roughly every 12 hours — worst case is a document arriving just after a submission: cadence (12h) + batch window (24h) = 36h, exactly at the boundary; any longer cadence breaks the SLA
Every 36 hours
Cadence doesn't matter if batches are submitted reliably
rationales: 24 + 24 = 48 hours worst case — twelve hours over the deadline.
The arithmetic is mechanical: max cadence = deadline − batch worst case − processing buffer. The slowest record (arriving one second after a submission) sets the constraint, not the average.
36 + 24 = 60 hours worst case.
Cadence is precisely the variable that determines the worst-case wait.

stem: When is a once-a-day batch submission actually safe?
options: Whenever volume is high enough to fill a batch
Always — daily is the standard cadence
Only when the SLA is at least 48 hours (24h max wait for the next submission + 24h batch window) and you can absorb tail latency
Only on weekends when traffic is low
rationales: Volume doesn't change the deadline math.
"Standard" cadences miss SLAs that are tighter than their worst case.
A record arriving just after today's submission waits ~24 hours, then up to 24 more in processing. Under a 48-hour SLA there is zero margin; under anything less, daily is unsafe.
Day of week has nothing to do with it.

stem: 3% of a 50,000-document batch failed — some with `context_length_exceeded`, some with validation errors. What now?
options: Rerun the whole batch — consistency matters
Ignore failures below 5%
Switch the failed documents to the real-time API at full price

Resubmit only the failures, handled by type: chunk the context-length failures and merge partials; resubmit validation failures with validation-error feedback; refine the prompt for prompt/schema issues
rationales: Re-running 48,500 successful documents doubles their cost for zero benefit.
Three percent of 50,000 is 1,500 real documents someone needed.
Real-time pricing doesn't fix why they failed — the same errors recur at higher cost.
Each failure type has a targeted remedy, applied only to affected `custom_id`s. Expired or canceled results likewise get resubmitted individually.

stem: Which operational practices matter when running large batches?
options: Stream/process the JSONL results incrementally; validate the request shape against the standard Messages API first — one malformed request won't fail the batch, it produces a per-request error you must reconcile; expect platform limits to force multiple batches
Load all results into memory for atomic processing
Skip request validation — the batch API validates for you
Submit everything as one batch regardless of size
rationales: Three unglamorous practices that prevent three expensive surprises: memory blowups, silent per-request failures, and rejected oversized submissions.
Large-job results are exactly what incremental processing exists for.
The batch accepts malformed requests and reports them as per-request errors after the fact — pre-validation is cheaper.
Batch size and request counts have platform limits; large pipelines need multiple batches.

stem: Can the batch discount and prompt caching stack, and what is the caveat?
options: No — the two mechanisms are mutually exclusive

Yes — batched requests support caching, so a shared prefix can be both cached and discounted; but cache hits inside an async batch are best-effort since requests may process far apart in time, and neither lever fixes the other's limits
Yes, and stacking removes the 24-hour window
Only with the five-minute TTL
rationales: They compose — that's part of the cost-lever playbook.
Stack them when the workload has both a repeated prefix and deferrable volume. But caching still doesn't enlarge the window or speed the batch, and the discount doesn't help a result needed now.
Nothing removes the 24-hour ceiling.
TTL choice affects hit rates, not whether stacking is possible.

stem: A team chooses batch processing purely because it's half price. What did they skip?
options: Nothing — cost is the deciding factor
The model selection analysis
The latency and SLA analysis — batch fit is decided by whether the workflow tolerates up to 24 hours, and no discount rescues a missed deadline
The prompt engineering review
rationales: Cost is one axis; the deadline axis vetoes.
Model tier is orthogonal to the batch decision.
Latency and SLA dominate the choice. Half price on results that arrive too late is a 100% loss.
Prompts transfer unchanged between batch and real-time.

stem: A batch "ended," but some results show `expired`. What is the correct handling?
options: Resubmit only the incomplete `custom_id`s in a new batch
Treat expiry as permanent data loss
Resubmit the entire original batch
Escalate to support — expiry indicates a platform fault
rationales: Individual results can succeed, error, be canceled, or expire — the response to incompletes is always targeted resubmission by ID.
Nothing is lost; the requests simply didn't process in the window.
Re-paying for every successful result to recover a few expired ones.
Expiry is a defined outcome to handle in code, not an incident.
